worth_it_explanation: Ad pod configuration sets how a Connected TV (CTV) ad break is structured, including how long the pod runs, how many ads it holds, and what plays back to back. Defaults left in place can run a break that is too long, too repetitive, or that pairs two competing brands in the same break, all of which wear on the viewer and on the demand that wants to reach them. This check reads the pod settings a publisher already has in OpenX rather than rebuilding the video stack, so it costs review time, and each finding points to a specific pod control the publisher can adjust.
intro: On CTV and over-the-top (OTT) inventory, ads play inside ad pods, which are the sequential ad breaks that run before, during, or after the content. The pod settings decide how long a break runs, how many ads it holds, how those ad slots are structured, and which ads are allowed to run next to each other. This subcategory is about whether those pod settings are deliberate choices that fit the content and protect the viewing experience, rather than platform defaults carried forward without review. These checks look at the pod structure itself and at the separation and frequency rules that govern what plays inside each break.

audit_checks.title: Pod duration, ad count, and slot structure are left at platform defaults
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Pod settings that carry the platform default control the length and density of every CTV break. A default that runs a long pod or packs many short ads into one break can exceed what the content and the audience tolerate, which raises abandonment and depresses the value of the inventory, so settings the publisher never reviewed shape revenue on every stream.

educate.base:
An ad pod is the ad break that runs inside Connected TV (CTV) and over-the-top (OTT) content, holding a sequence of ads that play one after another. The pod settings decide the shape of that break. Pod duration sets how long the whole break runs. The number of ad slots sets how many ads can fill it. The slot structure sets how those slots are divided, for example a single 30-second slot versus two 15-second slots in the same 30 seconds of break time. In Prebid-based video setups, these map to the ad pod request parameters, where the pod duration and the allowed ad durations together determine how many placements the break opens.

The risk in carrying the defaults forward is that the default is built for the platform, not for the content. A documentary with long scenes tolerates a different break rhythm than a fast-cut highlight reel. A default pod that runs long, or one that packs several short ads into a single break, can run past what the viewer will sit through, and on CTV the cost of that is the viewer leaving the stream rather than skipping a single ad. A break that wears on the audience also wears on the demand that paid to reach them. The later slots in a long pod reach a thinner audience as viewers drop off.

Pod duration and ad count are also a lever on yield, not only on experience. A longer pod with more slots opens more inventory per break, which can raise revenue per stream, but only up to the point where the added length starts costing completed views. The deliberate choice balances the two, and that balance depends on the content and the audience rather than on a number the platform shipped with.

A few signs point to pod settings carried forward as defaults rather than chosen:
  • Pod duration and ad-slot counts are identical across content types that differ in length and pacing
  • The publisher cannot state why a pod runs the length it does or holds the number of ads it holds
  • Completion rates fall sharply across the slots of a pod, which suggests the pod runs longer than the audience tolerates
You may want to confirm that pod duration, ad count, and slot structure were set to fit the content rather than accepted as defaults. One approach is to read completion rate by slot position within the pod, then shorten or restructure the pods where the later slots show heavy drop-off.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media property running CTV or OTT content, break rhythm depends on the program. A long-form documentary or a full episode tolerates a longer mid-roll pod than a short news clip or a highlight reel. You may want to set pod duration and ad count per content type rather than applying one pod shape to the whole catalog, so the break fits the program the viewer chose to watch.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-driven property with shoppable or product video, the viewer came for the content and a heavy ad break competes with the reason they arrived. You may want to keep pods on this inventory short and the ad count low, so the break does not push a shopping-intent viewer away before they reach the product.

educate.learn_more_note: OpenX's detailed configuration documentation for CTV pod settings is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review the pod duration, ad-slot count, and slot structure across the CTV and OTT inventory, and check whether those settings differ by content type or apply uniformly.

openx_ui.label: OpenX CTV inventory settings
steps: Open the CTV or video inventory configuration in OpenX and locate the ad pod settings for each placement or content type.
Record the pod duration, the number of ad slots, and the slot structure (for example one long slot or several short slots) for each placement.
Note whether these settings differ across content types that differ in length and pacing, or whether one pod shape applies across all CTV inventory.
Ask the publisher who set the pod values and on what basis, and record whether a reason beyond the default is on file.

reporting.label: Pod completion reporting
steps: Build a report that breaks out ad completion rate by slot position within the pod, for example first slot, second slot, and so on.
Compare completion across slot positions to see whether viewers drop off as the pod runs longer.
Note any content type where the later slots show heavy drop-off, which points to a pod that runs longer than the audience tolerates.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the pod duration, ad-slot count, and slot structure for each CTV placement alongside the completion rate by slot position. A setup where the pod settings were chosen to fit the content and the later slots hold their completion rate passes. A setup where the platform defaults carry across all content with no stated reason, or where completion falls sharply across the pod slots, is a fail recorded with the placements and pod settings affected.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the pod duration, ad count, and slot structure for each placement and the completion rate by slot position above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} runs its Connected TV (CTV) ad pods on platform-default duration, ad count, and slot structure, with the same pod shape applied across content types that differ in length and pacing. A default pod is built for the platform rather than the content, so a break that runs long or packs in too many slots can push viewers off the stream and thin out the audience the later slots reach. {{voice_recommend}} reading completion rate by slot position within the pod, then setting pod duration and ad count per content type so the break fits the program rather than a shipped default. After the change, recheck completion across the pod slots to confirm the later slots hold their audience.

audit_checks.title: Competitive separation and in-pod frequency are not enforced
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Without separation and frequency rules, a single pod can run two competing brands back to back or repeat the same ad within one break. That degrades the viewing experience and undercuts the demand paying for the inventory. An advertiser placed next to a direct competitor, or shown twice in one break, gets less value than the placement promised, which weakens the publisher's standing with that demand.

educate.base:
Inside an ad pod, the question is not only how many ads run but which ads run next to each other. Competitive separation keeps two brands in the same product category out of the same break, so a viewer does not see one car ad followed immediately by a competitor's car ad. In-pod frequency keeps the same ad, or the same advertiser, from repeating inside a single pod. Both rules protect the viewer from a break that feels repetitive or jarring, and both protect the advertiser who paid to reach that viewer in a clean placement.

On a sell-side platform (SSP) such as OpenX, enforcing separation across a pod is the platform's responsibility when more than one demand source fills the break, and no single buyer can see what the others are bidding. The IAB Tech Lab guidance describes how a platform applies these rules by reading the advertiser domain (the 'adomain' field) and the content category (the 'cat' field) on each bid response, then de-duplicating or separating ads that share an advertiser or a category. The VAST CTV Addendum ties this to a registered creative ID, which lets frequency capping and competitive separation work across the pod and across platforms by identifying the same creative wherever it appears.

When these rules are off or incomplete, the pod can run a category clash or a repeat without anyone catching it in the moment. The viewer notices, and the advertiser notices when their ad lands next to a competitor or plays twice in one break. On CTV, where the audience cannot skip and the break is a captive few minutes, a clash or a repeat carries more weight than it would in a skippable web pre-roll, so the separation rules matter more here, not less.

A few signs point to separation and frequency rules that are not enforced:
  • Pod settings have no competitive-separation rule keyed to advertiser domain or content category
  • No in-pod frequency rule prevents the same advertiser or creative from repeating within one break
  • Spot checks of recorded breaks show two same-category brands back to back or the same ad twice in one pod
You may want to confirm that the pod enforces both category separation and in-pod frequency, keyed to the advertiser domain and the content category on each bid. One approach is to review a sample of recorded breaks for clashes and repeats, then turn on or tighten the separation and frequency rules where the sample shows gaps.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media property, premium CTV inventory is part of what the publisher sells on its direct relationships, and a pod that clashes two competitors or repeats an ad undercuts the value the publisher promised those buyers. You may want to hold separation and frequency rules tightest on the premium content, so the placements the publisher charges the most for stay clean.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-driven property with product or shoppable video, a category clash inside the pod can pair the property's own retail interest against a competing advertiser, or repeat a product ad in a way that reads as pushy. You may want to confirm separation and frequency rules cover the product categories that matter most to the property's own commerce, so the break does not work against it.

learn_more.source_title: VAST CTV Addendum for Public Comment

educate.learn_more_note: OpenX's detailed configuration documentation for competitive separation and pod frequency is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review whether the pod enforces competitive separation and in-pod frequency, and check a sample of recorded breaks for category clashes or repeated ads.

openx_ui.label: OpenX CTV pod settings
steps: Open the CTV pod or video break configuration in OpenX and locate the competitive-separation and frequency settings.
Record whether a separation rule is enforced and what it keys on, for example advertiser domain (the 'adomain' field) or content category (the 'cat' field).
Record whether an in-pod frequency rule limits how often one advertiser or one creative can appear within a single break.
Note any pod or placement where separation or frequency is off, and the inventory it covers.

break_review.label: Recorded break review
steps: Collect a sample of recorded ad breaks across the CTV inventory, covering the highest-value placements.
Watch each sampled break for two same-category brands running back to back, which points to separation not being enforced.
Watch each sampled break for the same advertiser or the same creative appearing more than once, which points to in-pod frequency not being enforced.
Record each clash or repeat with the placement and the break it appeared in.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether competitive separation and in-pod frequency are enforced for each CTV placement, what the separation rule keys on, and what a sample of recorded breaks showed. A setup that enforces both rules, keyed to advertiser domain and content category, and shows no clashes or repeats in the sample passes. A setup with separation or frequency off, or with clashes or repeats found in the sample, is a fail recorded with the placements and breaks affected.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether separation and in-pod frequency are enforced and what the recorded-break sample showed above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} does not enforce competitive separation and in-pod frequency across its Connected TV (CTV) ad pods, so a single break can run two same-category brands back to back or repeat one advertiser within the pod. On a sell-side platform, separation across the pod is the platform's job when more than one demand source fills the break, applied by reading the advertiser domain and content category on each bid. {{voice_recommend}} turning on or tightening the separation and frequency rules, keyed to advertiser domain and content category, and reviewing a sample of recorded breaks to confirm the clashes and repeats stop. After the change, keep the rules tightest on the premium inventory where a clean placement carries the most value.
